content.text: Alert noise is the single largest drain on on-call morale and the primary reason engineers start ignoring pages. When a GenAI service goes to production it typically inherits a combination of infrastructure alerts (CPU, memory, network), application alerts (error rate, latency), and AI-specific alerts (eval drift, rate limits, cost) — many of which fire on the same incident and many of which fire when nothing is wrong.
Alert noise reduction is not a one-time cleanup; it is a
with a four-stage structure: audit what fires, suppress what is redundant, adjust thresholds to match real SLO burn, and review weekly to keep the signal clean.

attrs.alt: Four-stage alert noise reduction funnel — from 127 raw firings to 31 actionable alerts

content.text: Before tuning anything, measure what you have. Pull the last 30 days of alert history and classify each firing:
On-call took a remediation step and it resolved user harm
On-call investigated, found no user harm, spent > 5 min
Raise threshold or add for: duration
Alert fired, no investigation was warranted
Multiple alerts fired for the same root cause
Write inhibit rules or merge into one alert
Alert covers a feature that no longer exists

A practical way to classify: query Alertmanager's API for all resolved alerts and their duration, then join with on-call logs (PagerDuty annotations, Slack threads) to find which ones generated a human response.

import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

AM_URL = "https://alertmanager.example.com"
LOOKBACK_DAYS = 30

def fetch_alert_history(lookback_days: int = 30) -> list[dict]:
    start = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(days=lookback_days)
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{AM_URL}/api/v2/alerts",
        params={
            "active": "false",
            "silenced": "false",
            "inhibited": "false",
            "filter": 'env="prod"',
            "startTime": start.isoformat(),
        },
        timeout=30,
    )
    return resp.json()

def classify_alert(alert: dict, oncall_actions: set[str]) -> str:
    fingerprint = alert["fingerprint"]
    duration_s = (
        datetime.fromisoformat(alert["endsAt"].replace("Z", "+00:00"))
        - datetime.fromisoformat(alert["startsAt"].replace("Z", "+00:00"))
    ).total_seconds()

    if fingerprint in oncall_actions:
        return "actionable"
    if duration_s < 120:          # fired and resolved in < 2 min
        return "false_positive"
    if duration_s > 3600:         # fired for > 1 hour without action
        return "noisy"
    return "unknown"

alerts = fetch_alert_history()
# oncall_actions: set of fingerprints that appear in PagerDuty incident logs
from collections import Counter
classes = Counter(classify_alert(a, oncall_actions=set()) for a in alerts)
print(classes)
is already firing. For GenAI services the most common parent-child relationships are:
(child) — if the provider is down, every service using it will fire high-error-rate alerts independently
— rate limiting causes request queuing which causes latency; alerting on both is noise
inhibits all GenAI service alerts — infrastructure failure explains all application symptoms

# alertmanager/config.yaml
inhibit_rules:
  # Provider outage silences per-service error rate alerts
  - source_matchers:
      - alertname="GenAIProviderOutage"
      - severity="critical"
    target_matchers:
      - alertname=~"GenAI.*ErrorRate"
    equal: [env, provider]

  # Rate limit saturation silences latency alerts
  - source_matchers:
      - alertname="GenAIRateLimitSaturation"
    target_matchers:
      - alertname="GenAIHighLatency"
    equal: [env, model]

  # Node failure silences all application alerts on that node
  - source_matchers:
      - alertname="KubernetesNodeNotReady"
      - severity="critical"
    target_matchers:
      - job=~"genai.*"
    equal: [node]
Grouping reduces notification volume by batching related alerts into a single notification. The key insight: group by
. Grouping by alertname means every distinct alert fires a separate notification even when they all have the same cause.

# alertmanager/routing.yaml
route:
  group_by: ['env', 'team', 'severity']   # batch same-team same-severity alerts
  group_wait: 30s        # wait 30s before sending, collecting related alerts
  group_interval: 5m     # re-notify at most every 5m for ongoing group
  repeat_interval: 4h    # re-page at most every 4h if nothing changes
Most false-positive and noisy alerts share the same root causes: thresholds that are too low,
durations that are too short, or raw counts instead of rates.

Low-traffic services generate misleading error rates. A single failed request out of 5 total is a 20% error rate — enough to fire any reasonable alert — but the impact is one user, not a systemic failure.

# Bad: fires on 1 error out of 5 requests
- alert: GenAIHighErrorRate
  expr: |
    sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total[5m]))
      /
    sum(rate(genai_requests_total[5m]))
    > 0.01

# Good: add a minimum request floor
- alert: GenAIHighErrorRate
  expr: |
    (
      sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
        /
      sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
      > 0.01
    )
    and
    sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[5m])) > 1   # at least 1 req/s
alert fires the instant the expression evaluates true. For a 5-minute rate window, this means a single anomalous scrape can trigger a page. Almost all threshold alerts should use
5m burn rate window + 5m for: = genuine sustained issue
Long window avoids waking someone for a blip
Spend already incurred; no for: needed
Eval pipelines are async; a 1-hour drop may be pipeline lag
Before deploying a revised alert rule, backtest it against 30 days of Prometheus data using
. This shows how many times the rule would have fired and for how long:

# Backtest a revised alert rule against stored TSDB data
promtool tsdb analyze /var/prometheus/data --duration 720h

# Use the HTTP API to replay a query over a time range
curl -s "https://prometheus.example.com/api/v1/query_range" \
  --data-urlencode 'query=
    (
      sum(rate(genai_request_errors_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
      / sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[5m]))
      > 0.025
    ) and sum(rate(genai_requests_total{env="prod"}[5m])) > 1
  ' \
  --data-urlencode "start=$(date -d '30 days ago' +%s)" \
  --data-urlencode "end=$(date +%s)" \
  --data-urlencode "step=60" \
  | jq '.data.result | length'   # count of time series that would have fired
Planned maintenance (model upgrades, index rebuilds, provider migrations) causes predictable alert storms. Create
via the Alertmanager API before the maintenance starts:

import requests
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

AM_URL = "https://alertmanager.example.com"

def create_maintenance_silence(
    team: str,
    reason: str,
    duration_hours: int = 2,
    matchers: list[dict] | None = None,
) -> str:
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    silence = {
        "matchers": matchers or [
            {"name": "team", "value": team, "isRegex": False},
        ],
        "startsAt": now.isoformat(),
        "endsAt": (now + timedelta(hours=duration_hours)).isoformat(),
        "createdBy": "deployment-automation",
        "comment": reason,
    }
    resp = requests.post(
        f"{AM_URL}/api/v2/silences",
        json=silence,
        timeout=10,
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    silence_id = resp.json()["silenceID"]
    print(f"Silence created: {silence_id} (expires in {duration_hours}h)")
    return silence_id

# Called from deploy pipeline before a model upgrade
create_maintenance_silence(
    team="genai-oncall",
    reason="Scheduled model upgrade gpt-4o → gpt-4o-2024-11 (deploy-789)",
    duration_hours=1,
    matchers=[
        {"name": "env", "value": "prod", "isRegex": False},
        {"name": "team", "value": "genai-oncall", "isRegex": False},
        {"name": "alertname", "value": "GenAI.*", "isRegex": True},
    ],
)
Noise reduction is not a project; it is a standing process. Three cadences maintain alert quality:
Every Monday, the on-call lead reviews the prior week's alert history in a 15-minute sync:
How many alerts fired? How many led to on-call action?
Were there any missed incidents (symptoms without alerts)?
Were there any alerts that fired during known-healthy periods?
Are any alerts in a permanent 'acknowledged but not fixed' state?
as a KPI: the fraction of alerts that resulted in a documented on-call action. Target ≥ 80 %. Below 50 % is an alert hygiene emergency.

# Alert-to-action rate proxy: alerts that resolved within an expected window
# (proxy: alerts that were not silenced and resolved in < 30m)
sum(
  alertmanager_alerts_resolved_total{env="prod", silenced="false"}
    and on() alertmanager_alert_groups_resolved_total < 1800
)
  /
sum(alertmanager_alerts_resolved_total{env="prod"})
After every incident add an alert review to the post-mortem template:
detected the incident? How long after the user impact started?
Action items: add, modify, or delete specific alert rules
The post-incident alert review is the highest-signal tuning input. A real incident reveals gaps and false positives that no amount of synthetic testing finds. Make it mandatory.

Each quarter, identify and remove alerts that have not fired in 90 days. An alert that never fires is one of two things: a dead metric (the code that emits it was removed) or a threshold so high it will never trigger. Either way it is dead weight that clutters the config:
: a maximum number of pages per on-call shift. Google SRE recommends a maximum of 2 incidents per on-call shift. For GenAI services, a reasonable starting budget is
≤ 5 page-worthy alerts per week per team
When the budget is exceeded, the
fires — and the response is to tune alerts, not to accept more noise:

# Meta-alert: alert budget exceeded
- alert: AlertBudgetExceeded
  expr: |
    sum(
      increase(alertmanager_notifications_total{
        integration="pagerduty",
        team="genai-oncall"
      }[7d])
    ) > 5
  for: 0m
  labels:
    severity: warning
    team: observability-leads   # routes to the observability team, not the on-call
  annotations:
    summary: 'GenAI on-call received {{ $value }} pages this week (budget: 5)'
    description: 'Alert tuning needed. Review the weekly alert history.'
Several noise patterns are specific to GenAI services:
P99 spikes when the first request hits a cold instance
Quality alert fires because eval results are 2h delayed, not because quality degraded
Shift eval alert windows to match pipeline latency (2h+)
Provider posts maintenance, your alerts fire before the silence is created
Subscribe to provider status RSS; auto-create silences via webhook
Token count metric changes after a tokenizer library update; rate-limit alerts fire
Pin tokenizer version in CI; alert on tokenizer version change
Nightly batch job exhausts hourly token quota; latency alert fires
Exclude batch job labels from user-facing latency alert; alert separately on batch quota

Alert noise reduction follows a four-stage discipline: audit and classify existing alerts, write inhibit and grouping rules to suppress redundancy, tune thresholds using burn rates and minimum floors, then review weekly to maintain signal quality.
Classify every alert as actionable, noisy, false-positive, duplicate, or expired before tuning
Use Alertmanager inhibit_rules to suppress child alerts when a parent (provider outage, node failure) is already firing
Always add a minimum request floor to error rate expressions to avoid firing on low-traffic noise
Backtest revised thresholds against 30 days of Prometheus data before deploying
Set an explicit alert budget (≤ 5 pages/week per team) and treat budget violations as tuning signals
Post-incident alert reviews are the highest-signal input for ongoing threshold calibration
